concepts.title: Definition of one-one,many-one,into and onto mappings
concepts.conceptBody:
Many-one, one-one onto and into mappings

Let $ ext{f}: ext{X} o ext{Y}$.

The mapping $ ext{f}$ is said to be many-one iff two or more different elements in $ ext{X}$ have the

same $ ext{f}$ image in $ ext{Y}$. The mapping $ ext{f}$ is said to be one-one iff different elements in $ ext{X}$ have

different $ ext{f}$-images in Y i.e. if ${{ ext{x}}_{1}} e {{ ext{x}}_{2}}\Rightarrow ext{f}\left( {{ ext{x}}_{1}} ight) e ext{f}\left( {{ ext{x}}_{2}} ight)$ or equivalently,

$ ext{f}\left( {{ ext{x}}_{1}} ight)= ext{f}\left( {{ ext{x}}_{2}} ight)\Rightarrow {{ ext{x}}_{1}}={{ ext{x}}_{2}}$. One-one mappings are also called injection. The mapping $f$ is

said to be into if there is at least one element in $Y$ which is not the f-image of any element in

$ ext{X}$. Note that in this case the range of $ ext{f}$ is a proper subset of $ ext{Y}$, that is $ ext{f}\left[ ext{X} ight]\subset ext{Y}$ and

$ ext{f}\left[ ext{X} ight] e ext{Y}$. The mapping $ ext{f}$ is said to be onto if every element in $ ext{Y}$ is the f-image of at least

one element in $ ext{X}$. In this case, the range of $ ext{f}$ is euqal to $ ext{Y}$, that is $ ext{f}\left[ ext{X} ight]= ext{Y}$. Onto

mapping are also called surjection . One-one and onto mappings are called bijection .

concepts.title: Discussion of One-One,Many-one,into,onto,Many-one onto,Many-one into,One-one into and One-one onto Mappings
concepts.conceptBody:
Illustration and introduction of words, 'one-one', 'many one' ‘onto and into’

Let $\text{A}$ be the set of books in a Library and B be the set of certain natural numbers. Let a, b, c, $\text{d},\ldots $. denote different books and let 240,320, 108, 50 etc. denote some elements of the set B which correspond to the number of pages in the books.

Now choose $f$ to be the correspondence which assigns to each book the number of pages contained in it ie. $\text{f}:\text{A}\to \text{B}$.

The following points should be clearly understood.

  1. Each book $\in \text{A}$ is associated to some number $\in \text{B}$ (ie. the number of pages in that book). This number will be the image of the corresponding book.
  2. Two or more books may be associated to the same number $\in \text{B}$ (i.e. Two or more of books may have the same number of pages).

In this case it will be termed as many one function or mapping as two or more elements $\in \text{A}$ will have the same image $\in \text{B}$ or an element $\in \text{B}$ will have more than one pre-image in $\text{A}$.

  1. No book can be associated to different elements of B, i.e. the same book cannot have different number of pages in it i.e. each book is associated to a unique number $\in \text{B}$ i.e. the image of each and every book is unique. 
  2. If all the books $\in \text{A}$ are associated to different numbers $\in \text{B}$ i.e. all the books have different number of pages i.e. all the elements of $A$ have different $f$ images in $B$ or an element of $B$ has only one pre-image in A then this mapping is said to be one-one mapping.
  3. There may be certain numbers in B which do not represent the number of pages of any of the books $\in \text{A}$ then the mapping is said to be into mapping i.e. $\text{f}$ is a mapping of $\text{A}$ into $\text{B}$. In other words there is at least one element $\in \text{B}$ which is not the image of any element $\in \text{A}$ then $f$ is a mapping from $A$ into $B$. In this case the set of images i.e. range of $f$ is a subset of B.
  4. Now suppose each number $\in \text{B}$ represents the number of pages of at least one book $\in $ to $\text{A}$ then the mapping $f$ is said to be onto mapping i.e. $f:A$ 'onto'  B. 

In this case each and every element of set $\text{B}$ is the image of at least one element in $\text{A}$. The set $\text{B}$ i.e. co-domain is completely covered by the $f$ images of the domain $A$ and consequently $f\left( A \right)$ i.e. the set of images $=\text{B}$.

Many-one onto mapping - When two or more books $\in \text{A}$ have the same number of pages i.e. the same image $\in \text{B}$ (i.e. many-one) and also each and every number $\in \text{B}$ represent the number of pages of at least one book (i.e. onto) then $\text{f}:\text{A}\to \text{B}$ is a many-one mapping.

Many-one into mapping - When two or more books $\in \text{A}$ have the same number of pages i.e. the same image $\in \text{B}$ (i.e. many-one) and there are certain numbers $\in \text{B}$ which do not represent the number of pages of any of the books $\in \text{A}$ i.e. (into) then $\text{f}:\text{A}\to \text{B}$ is a many-one into mapping.

One-one into mapping - When all the books $\in \text{A}$ are having different number of pages i.e. they are associated to different numbers $\in \text{B}$ (one-one) and there are certain numbers $\in \text{B}$ which do not represent the number of pages of any of the books $\in $ A i.e. (into) then $f$ : $\text{A}\to \text{B}$ is a one-one into mapping.

One-one onto mapping - When all the books $\in \text{A}$ are having different number of pages i.e. they are associated to diferent numbers $\in \text{B}$ (one-one) and there is no number $\in \text{B}$ which does not represent the number of pages of a book $\in \text{A}$ i.e. each and every number $\in \text{B}$ represents the number of pages of a certain book $\in \text{A}$ (onto) then $\text{f}:\text{A}\to \text{B}$ is a one-one onto mapping. This is also called Bijection.

concepts.title: Types of mapping
concepts.conceptBody:
Type of Mappings 

(1) Injective mapping = one-one mapping - Non- (many-one mapping).

(2) Surjective mapping = onto mapping - Non (into mapping).

(3) Bijective mapping = inverse mapping (invertible)

Injective Mapping : 

Every element in co-domain should have at most one pre-image.

Checking injective mapping :

(1) By inspection 

eg. $y={{x}^{2}}=1,y=0$ at $x=\pm 1$

non-injective (many one mapping)

(2) Graphical Approach : 

Steps : (i) Draw graph of $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ in domain and co-domain.

(ii) Draw horizontal lines in co-domain.

(iii) Mark pts. of intersection $\text{b}/\text{w}$ graph.

(iv) If every horizontal line has atmost one pt. of intersection (0 or 1$)$ with the graph then mapping is injetive or else many one mapping.

eg. $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }y=\text{tan}x\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left( -\frac{\pi }{2},\frac{\pi }{2} \right)\to R\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }y={{x}^{2}}$

(3) Derivative method. (for continuous functions) 

If $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ has no pt. of (l max. or l-min) in domain i.e. either increasing/decreasing in domain then mapping is injective (one-one).

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ eg}\text{. }\!\!~\!\!\text{ y}=\text{log}\left( \text{logx} \right)\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left[ 1,\infty \right)\to \text{R}$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{1}{\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{x}}}\frac{1}{x}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ is }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }>0 & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ for }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }x\in \left( 1,\infty \right)\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ one-one }\!\!~\!\!\text{ mapping }\!\!~\!\!\text{ (increasing }\!\!~\!\!\text{ function) }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ e}\text{.g }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }y=\text{log}\left( \text{log}\left( \text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{\text{x}}} \right) \right. & \left( e,\infty \right)\to R \\ \frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{1}{\text{log}\left( \text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{x}} \right)}\frac{1}{\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{x}}}\times \frac{1}{x}>0 & x\in \left( e,\infty \right) \\ \end{array}$

Surjective Mapping : 

Every element is co-domain is a paired element or every element in co-domain has at

least one pre image.

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left( \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ codomain }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }=\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ Range }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \right)$

Bijection Mapping : 

For bijection, function has to be both injective and surjective

concepts.title: Methods to find number of one-one(injective),onto(surjective) and bijective mappings
concepts.conceptBody:
Method to find no. of mappings 

Number of one-one mappings 

If $\text{A}\And \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ B}$ are 2 finite sets having $\text{m}\And \text{n}$

elements respectively then, the number

of one-one functions from A to B are-

There are $\text{m}$ elements in set A & $\text{n}$ in set B.

For one-one mapping to occur $\text{n}\ge \text{m}$

Now, ${{x}_{1}}$ can take $n$ images

${{\text{x}}_{2}}$ can take $\left( \text{n}-1 \right)$ images, removing the one used by ${{x}_{1}}$ already.

Similarly ${{x}_{3}}$

${{\text{x}}_{\text{n}}}$ can take $\left( \text{n}-\text{m}+1 \right)$ images

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Total number of mappings $=\text{n }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left( \text{n}-1 \right)\left( \text{n}-2 \right)\ldots .\left( \text{n}-\text{m}+1 \right)$

$={{~}^{n}}{{P}_{m}}$

Mapping possible $\left\{ \begin{matrix} {{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{P}}_{\text{m}}} & ;\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ if }\!\!~\!\!\text{ n}\ge \text{m} \\ 0 & ;\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ if }\!\!~\!\!\text{ n}<\text{m} \\ \end{matrix} \right.$

Number of onto functions 

For this case you can just remember the formula or see a simple example given in

your package. For surjection from $\text{A}$ to $\text{B}$, where A contains $\text{m}$ and $\text{B}$ contains $\text{n}$ elements

The formula is $\sum _{\text{r}=1}^{\text{n}}{{(-1)}^{\text{n}-\text{r}}}{{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}{{(\text{r})}^{\text{m}}}$

Number of bijection mappings 

For a mapping to be bijective, it must be both, one-one & onto.

i.e. both sets should have same number of elements

 

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{x}}_{1}}$ can take $\left( \text{n} \right)$ images

${{\text{x}}_{2}}$ can ...... $\left( \text{n}-1 \right)$ images

${{\text{x}}_{\text{n}}}$ can take 1 image

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Total number of mappings

$=\text{n}\left( \text{n}-1 \right)\left( \text{n}-2 \right)\ldots 1$

$=n$ !

concepts.title: Determining injective,surjective and bijective functions
concepts.conceptBody:
Determining injective,surjective and bijective functions

Q) Let $\mathbf{A}=\left\{ \text{x}:-1\le \text{x}\le 1 \right\}=\mathbf{B}$ be a mapping $\text{f}:\mathbf{A}\to \mathbf{B}$. For each of the following 

functions from $A$ to $B$, find whether it is surjective or bijective. 
(a) $f\left( x \right)=\left| x \right|$ (b) $f\left( x \right)=\left| x \right|$ (c) $f\left( x \right)={{x}^{3}}$ 
(d) $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)=\left[ \text{x} \right]$ (e) $f\left( x \right)=\text{sin}\frac{\pi x}{2}$ 

Solution : 

(a) $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)=\left| \text{x} \right|$

Graphically;

Which shows many one, as the straight line is parallel to $\text{x}$-axis and cuts at two

points. Here range for $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)\in \left[ 0,1 \right]$

Which is clearly subset of co-domain.

i.e. $\left[ 0,1\left] \subseteq \right[-1,1 \right]$

Thus, into

Hence, function is many-one-into.

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ neither injective nor surjective.

 

(b) $f\left( x \right)=x\left| x \right|$,

Graphically,

The graph shows $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ is one-one,

as the straight line parallel to $\text{x}$-axis cuts only at one point.

Here, range

$\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)\in \left[ -1,1 \right]$

Thus, range $=$ co-domain

Hence, onto.

Therefore, $f\left( x \right)$ is one-one onto or (bijective).

 

(c) $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)={{\text{x}}^{3}}$,

Graphically;

Graph shows $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ is one-one onto

(i.e. bijective ).

[as explained in above example].

 

(d) $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)=\left[ \text{x} \right]$,

Graphically;

which shows $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ is many-one, as the straight line

parallel to $\text{x}$-axis meets at

more than one point.

Here, range

$\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)\in \left\{ -1,0,1 \right\}$

which shows into as range co-domain.

Hence, many-one-into. 

 

(e) $f\left( x \right)=\text{sin}\frac{\pi x}{2}$

Graphically,

which shows $f\left( x \right)$ is one-one and onto as range.

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ = }\!\!~\!\!\text{ co-domain}\text{. }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

Therefore, $f\left( x \right)$ is bijective.

Q) Find number of surjections from $A$ to $B$ where 

$A=\left\{ 1,2,3,4 \right\},B=\left\{ a,b \right\}$

Solution : 

Number of surjection from A to B

\(\begin{array}{l}=\sum_{\mathrm{r}=1}^2(-1)^{2-\mathrm{r}}{ }^2 \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}}(\mathrm{r})^4 \\ =(-1)^{2-1}{ }^2 \mathrm{C}_1(1)^4+(-1)^{2-2}{ }^2 \mathrm{C}_2(2)^4 \\ =-2+16 \\ =14\end{array}\)

Therefore, number of onto mapping from $\text{A}$ to $\text{B}=14$.

Alter : Total number of mapping from $A$ to $B$ is ${{2}^{4}}$ of which two function $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)=\text{a}$

for all $\text{x}\in \text{A}$ and $\text{g}\left( \text{x} \right)=\text{b}$ for all $\text{x}\in \text{A}$ are not surjective.

Thus, total number of surjection from $\text{A}$ to $\text{B}={{2}^{4}}-2$

$=14$
